key_claims.claim: A lens 'element' is an individual piece of glass, while a 'group' is either a single element or multiple elements cemented together without air space between them. A lens with 10 elements in 8 groups has two cemented pairs (doublets).

John Dollond patented the achromatic doublet in 1758, combining crown glass and flint glass to correct chromatic aberration. He received the Copley Medal from the Royal Society that same year, though Chester Moore Hall may have created similar lenses earlier.

Without anti-reflective coatings, each air-glass interface reflects about 4% of incident light. A complex lens with many elements could lose 70% of light to reflection and scatter. Simple 1935-era single-layer coatings reduced reflection at each surface from 4% to about 1%.

Alexander Smakula at Zeiss developed the first practical anti-reflective coating process in 1935, which remained a military secret through most of World War II. Allied forces coveted German binoculars because of this coating advantage.

The Tessar lens design, created by Paul Rudolph for Zeiss in 1902, uses 4 elements in 3 groups. The name derives from the Greek word for 'four.' Dozens of modern lenses are Tessar derivatives, including Leitz Elmars and Schneider Xenars.

The Sonnar lens was developed by Ludwig Bertele at Zeiss in 1930. The name comes from the German word 'Sonne' (sun), reflecting its ability to gather light with large apertures (f/2.0 and f/1.5). Bertele minimized air-glass surfaces by cementing elements together to avoid Fresnel losses before coatings existed.

Aspherical lens elements have a radius of curvature that varies from center to edge, unlike spherical elements which have a constant radius. A single aspherical element can often replace multiple spherical elements. In zoom lenses with 10+ elements, two aspherical lenses can substitute for several spherical ones.

Canon's Subwavelength Structure Coating (SWC) uses wedge-shaped aluminum oxide nanostructures only 220nm high—smaller than visible light wavelengths—to reduce reflection to around 0.05%. Canon's high-end lenses with 10+ coating layers achieve 99.9% light transmission from UV to near-infrared.

Canon released the FL-F300mm f/5.6 in May 1969—the world's first consumer telephoto lens with synthetic fluorite elements. Fluorite (calcium fluoride) has anomalous dispersion properties that correct chromatic aberration more effectively than glass, and is also lighter than optical glass.

There are six main types of optical aberrations: spherical aberration, coma, astigmatism, field curvature, distortion, and chromatic aberration. The first five are 'monochromatic' (present even with single-wavelength light); chromatic aberration is caused by dispersion.

ED (Extra-low Dispersion) glass has a high Abbe number, meaning low dispersion. When paired with standard optical glass, ED elements help bring red, green, and blue wavelengths to a common focus point—apochromatic correction. Nikon introduced ED glass in telephoto lenses starting in 1971.

Modern lenses increasingly rely on in-camera software correction for distortion and vignetting. Some wide-angle lenses show severe barrel distortion and multiple stops of vignetting in raw files, which is corrected automatically. This allows designers to optimize for other parameters like sharpness and size.

Lens design changed fundamentally around 1960 with digital computers and numerical optimization. Zemax, introduced in the early 1990s by Kenneth Moore, became one of the most widely used optical design programs. It was acquired by Ansys in 2021.

Aspherical lens manufacturing costs exceed spherical production by 35-50% due to equipment requirements and longer processing times. Aspherical production requires CNC machinery and diamond-turning lathes achieving surface accuracy within 0.1 microns.

Before coatings, lens designers favored designs that minimized air-glass surfaces because each interface caused significant flare and light loss. The development of anti-reflective coatings relaxed this constraint, allowing air-spaced elements to be used freely in modern designs.
